summary: American Postmodern architect (1925–2018), known for the quip 'Less is a bore.' Authored Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture and co-authored Learning from Las Vegas with his wife Denise Scott Brown and Steven Izenour. Key buildings include the Vanna House, the Sainsbury Wing of London's National Gallery, and the Seattle Art Museum. Won the 1991 Pritzker Prize.

works.name: Vanna House (Vanna Venturi House)
works.description: House built for Venturi's mother Vanna in Philadelphia. Features an exposed chimney, a wrap-around staircase, a gently sloping roof, and a large monumental facade with a split gable. Described by critic Frederick Schwartz as 'the first Postmodern anything.' Located near the Esherick House by Louis Kahn. Venturi lived in the house after marrying Denise Scott Brown. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Designed for his mother Vanna; exposed chimney, wrap-around staircase, sloping roof, monumental facade with a split gable; called 'the first Postmodern anything'
examples: A staircase surrounds the central chimney above a large facade created by a sloping roof in a home built for this man's mother, Vanna.
This architect created a house with an exposed chimney, a wrap-around staircase, and a gently sloping roof for his mother.
One structure designed by this architect was described by Frederick Schwartz as the 'first Postmodern anything' for its monumental facade and was designed for his mother Vanna in Philadelphia.

clues.clue: Venturi lived in the house after marrying Denise Scott Brown
examples: An architect from this city lived in a house he designed for his mother after marrying Denise Scott Brown.

works.name: Learning from Las Vegas
works.description: 1972 book co-authored by Venturi, his wife Denise Scott Brown, and Steven Izenour. Full title: 'A Significance for A&P Parking Lots, or Learning from Las Vegas.' Celebrated roadside decor and the Las Vegas strip. Divided buildings into two types: 'ducks' (where the shape represents the building's function) and 'decorated sheds.' The genesis for this work came from a study of A&P Parking Lots; Venturi led Yale students on helicopter tours dubbed the 'Great Proletarian Cultural Locomotive.' Its movement on Las Vegas neon signs was described as 'quicker than mosaic glitter.' Includes a section titled 'Space as God.' La Tourette Monastery used as an example. Led to the development of two opposing factions: the 'Greys' (who followed LfLV's principles) and the 'Whites' (who dissented — including Richard Meier and Michael Graves of the New York Five). Aron Vinegar's I am a Monument is a critical examination of this book. Wikipedia

clues.clue: 1972 manifesto co-authored with Denise Scott Brown and Steven Izenour; celebrated roadside decor and the Las Vegas strip; divided buildings into 'ducks' and 'decorated sheds'
examples: Steven Izenour, Denise Scott Brown, and this creator of the Vanna House wrote the book Learning from Las Vegas.
a book co-written by this man, his wife Denise Scott Brown, and Steven Izenour that celebrated roadside decor and the strip of the title city.
Buildings whose shape represents their purpose are termed 'ducks' and contrasted with 'decorated' architecture in a book titled for this city.

Following disagreements in the 1970s, these two colors were used to name the camps of postmodern architects who either followed Learning from Las Vegas's principles or, like the New York Five architects Richard Meier and Michael Graves, dissented.

works.name: Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture
works.description: 1966 book by Venturi that attacked modernism with a call for 'messy vitality' and an eclectic style. Defined his Postmodern philosophy in contrast to Mies van der Rohe's minimalism. Its 'nonstraightforward' introduction presents a 'gentle manifesto' citing Gödel's incompleteness theorem and T.S. Eliot's analysis of 'difficult' poetry as precedents. Analyzes 'double-functioning' and 'both-and' elements of buildings. Praises Philip Johnson's Wiley House but criticizes the 'bland architecture' of his Glass House. Praises Alvar Aalto for combining 'standardization with irrationality' in his Cultural Center in Wolfsburg. Argues buildings should rely on a technique called 'poche' instead of transparency. Yale historian Vincent Scully wrote the introduction. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Called for an eclectic style of 'messy vitality'; argued for 'ugly and ordinary architecture'; attacked modernism
examples: He called for an eclectic style of 'messy vitality' in his book Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture
This architect of the Lieb House argued for 'ugly and ordinary architecture' in a work that divided buildings into 'ducks' and 'decorated sheds.'

clues.clue: Vincent Scully wrote the introduction
examples: This scholar wrote the introduction to an architect's book that coined the maxim, 'less is a bore,' Robert Venturi's Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture.

works.name: Fire Station No. 4, Columbus, Indiana
works.description: Fire station designed by Venturi in Columbus, Indiana, featuring a giant yellow (gold) '4' at the top, with a hose-drying tower inscribed with the number placed in the middle separating equipment and living quarters, so the whole structure looks like an overturned number four. Financed by J. Irwin Miller, who championed the city (nicknamed 'Athens on the Prairie') as an international center of modernist architecture. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Fire station with a giant gold No. 4 at the top; hose-drying tower in the middle; whole structure resembles an overturned number four
examples: A giant yellow four appears at the top of a fire station designed by this architect in Columbus, Indiana.

works.name: Sainsbury Wing (National Gallery, London)
works.description: Addition to the National Gallery in London, designed with Denise Scott Brown. Denise Scott Brown refused to attend the 1991 Pritzker Prize ceremony because Venturi was awarded sole credit for the work on which they had collaborated. Also designed the Seattle Art Museum. Wikipedia

examples: This architect's partner on the Sainsbury Wing of London's National Gallery refused to attend his 1991 Pritzker Prize ceremony since he was given sole awardship on projects like the Seattle Art Museum.
He created a house that exposes its chimney...and bunched pilasters to create a concertina-like effect on the facade of a museum addition co-designed with his wife Denise Scott Brown.

works.name: Other Works / General
works.description: Other works include: the Lieb House (with a semicircle 'sailboat' window and a huge number nine beside the door; had to be floated up the East River to escape New Jersey); Franklin Court in Philadelphia (with two steel-tube 'ghost structures' in the shape of homes); the Allen Art Museum addition at Oberlin; the Brant-Johnson Ski House in Vail (built almost entirely of pine planks); the Haute-Garonne Capitol Building in Toulouse; the never-built Bill-Ding-Building for the NFL Hall of Fame in New Brunswick; a bank designed for the Disney-sponsored planned town of Celebration, Florida. His quip 'Less is a bore' plays on Mies van der Rohe's 'Less is more.' Louis Kahn was his teacher. His favorite building in America was Sever Hall by H.H. Richardson at Harvard.

comprehensive_summary:
Robert Venturi (1925–2018) was the foremost American Postmodern architect, best known for the quip 'Less is a bore,' a pointed rebuke of Mies van der Rohe's Modernist dictum 'Less is more.' He won the Pritzker Prize in 1991, though his long-time partner and wife Denise Scott Brown refused to attend the ceremony because the prize was awarded to him alone for projects on which they had collaborated, including the Sainsbury Wing of London's National Gallery. Louis Kahn was Venturi's teacher; both worked in Philadelphia, and Venturi's most celebrated building sits near Kahn's Esherick House.

His most celebrated building is the Vanna House (Vanna Venturi House) in Philadelphia, designed for his mother Vanna. It features an exposed chimney, a wrap-around staircase, a gently sloping roof, and a monumental split-gable facade. Critic Frederick Schwartz called it 'the first Postmodern anything.' Venturi himself later lived in the house after marrying Denise Scott Brown.

Venturi co-authored two foundational texts of Postmodern architecture. Complexity and Contradiction in Architecture (1966) called for an eclectic 'messy vitality' against Modernist purity. Its 'nonstraightforward' introduction presents a 'gentle manifesto' citing Gödel's incompleteness theorem and T.S. Eliot as precedents. The book analyzes 'double-functioning' and 'both-and' elements of buildings, argues for 'poche' over transparency, praises Philip Johnson's Wiley House while criticizing his Glass House, and praises Alvar Aalto for combining 'standardization with irrationality.' Vincent Scully, the Yale architectural historian, wrote the introduction.

Learning from Las Vegas (1972, full title: A Significance for A&P Parking Lots, or Learning from Las Vegas), written with Denise Scott Brown and Steven Izenour, celebrated Las Vegas roadside commercial architecture and introduced the distinction between 'ducks' (buildings whose form represents their function) and 'decorated sheds.' The genesis came from a study of A&P parking lots; Venturi led Yale students on helicopter tours of the city (jokingly dubbed the 'Great Proletarian Cultural Locomotive'), describing the movement of neon signs as 'quicker than mosaic glitter.' The book includes a section titled 'Space as God' and uses La Tourette Monastery as an example. Its publication split the architectural world into 'Greys' (who followed its principles) and 'Whites' (who dissented, including New York Five architects Richard Meier and Michael Graves). Aron Vinegar's I am a Monument is a critical examination of that book.

Other notable projects include Fire Station No. 4 in Columbus, Indiana — financed by J. Irwin Miller, the patron who turned Columbus ('Athens on the Prairie') into a center of modernist architecture. The fire station has a giant gold '4' making the structure look like an overturned number four. The Lieb House featured a 'sailboat' semicircle window, the number nine beside the door, and had to be floated up the East River to escape New Jersey. Franklin Court in Philadelphia used two steel-tube 'ghost structures' in the shape of homes. Venturi also designed a bank in the Disney-sponsored planned town of Celebration, Florida alongside Cesar Pelli. His favorite building in America was H.H. Richardson's Sever Hall at Harvard.
